speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about going back and forth in the ghetto. How often did you go back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, each day I was there. Because later there, like I said before, Mr. Melamed parents has difficulty to have some representative in Aryan part for the selling the toys. I took this part. And I sell the toys all over the Warsaw for a big supplier in other words. The toys was produced in the ghetto from the wood. And I not think so. Was problem with the supply of the material. I mean the wood still was traditionally done, all the time, in this area where was ghetto. And each third days, four days, I was sent big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wagon, you know, because it's light stuff, a big wagon to Warsaw, with each package, which was separate, and sent to the store. Some store called Mr. Birnbaum, directly called, because telephone was free. Not free, only was possible to talk. Some, I pick up the order and sent-- bring it to Mr. Birnbaum. And later there, after it was delivered to the place, I collect the money. And I bring the money to Birnbaum and to meet family, Melamed and so. And that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was almost each day in the ghetto. To be in ghetto and meet the people was fine. Only go through the street was terrible. Sometimes you go 100 feet, and you see 5, 6, sometimes more people dying, dying on the street. That was the worst part what you can see. And the part of the-- in ghetto was organization where, you know, policemen supposed to keep the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in other words, keep the quiet there. And also, on the other hand, the policemen has the obligation-- obligation from German, bring-- later there, bring 10,000 people to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did policeman, Jewish policeman did? Take first the dead people from the street and count, one, two, three, up to x-number thousand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, there was less people dying on the street, pick up the other people. And everybody, later there, we found was sent to Treblinka, to concentration camp. Because supposed to be sent-- German asked, send us 10,000 people to east for work. That was lie, not true. And these people were sent, later there, to concentration camp, east part.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was my visit in a ghetto. And, you know, after you were so many times, you know, the people ask you for some favor. What I did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each day I go there, I bring something in my pocket that was possibly not visible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Germans cannot catch me. And two time, the German catch me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was not visible that you put in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, for example, you can put a chocolate or a little bit sugar or-- or a something. If you put a big apple, is visible, right? If you put 10 kartoffel, for example, potato, is visible, because it's big. You can-- or you-- you can put, for example, a lunch, what-- own lunch there. That's all right. That's for probably nobody complained. You not see this. That's not visible, I call. And two times, I was caught by gendarmes and sent to Polish precinct-- Polish precinct. And my father was-- has connection with was some people, some way and pay. One times, it was not so bad. Other times, pay $4,000-- 4,000 zloty. And this time was big, big money. And that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to go back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, listen, that was-- later, there, you know, was some obligation. I already worked with these people. And I must bring something. I mean bring for father something. And you know, it never was the same guy, [LAUGHS] on the same place. I mean German. Jewish people not-- not search me. Polish policeman not search me, only German. The gendarmes, these people, was-- we was afraid. And never the same time, the same day-- next day, the gendarmes was in the same place. In other words, we learned this automatic. Going back and forth, you know is not possible. You go in different day or different hour. And I go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of conditions did you see when you went into the Melameds' place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Melamed, you know, was very, very nice people. And this place, where was the business, was clean. And was all the time polite, you know? That you not see the ghetto, really. And Melamed sell everything what has, keep the family together, and buy on the black market some-- something we bring to these people. And some-- mostly, the people buy on the black market there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was terrible prices. And still, you know, sell everything what the fellow has. He was a pretty wealthy man. And was a good father, you know? [LAUGHS] You must. What is good if you have 10 dresses, and you are hungry or your children are hungry? That's the fellow did, you know? That's sell and for half-price sometime, something, and support his family in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melamed's place, you not see the hunger. Bad hunger was, you know, big different before the war, before 1939, and before the ghetto was closed. Is big-- there was big different after the ghetto was closed, I mean tight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my goodness, if I tell you it closed-- closed means destroyed, right? Maybe we said so. That was-- that was very important for us day, during the bombardment of ghetto, not-- artillery bombardment ghetto and fire in the ghetto. That was Sunday. We sat down to eat dinner. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOB] terrible feeling. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] A very, very difficult situation. Nobody touch eat. [DRINKS] [SIGHS] [SIGHS] The whole ghetto was burning. On the outside, it was like in Berlin wall. And on the other side, you see, on the top balcony, people was hanging. That was not only Jewish people. That was prisoner what was in ghetto. Because there was some prisoner, political prisoner, Polish side and all over. And maybe 10, maybe 15 people, you know, was tied and like this and hanging in the wind, you know, blowing. If you see this, that's terrible. And, you know, this day, I remember very well. Dinner was served in on our house. And this time, we live closer to ghetto. Because other, old house was destroyed. And we moved to the Miodowa-Strasse, Miodowa Street. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the smell of the burning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] buildings was terrible smell. And you know, you see the shooting, bombard-- artillery bombardment. You know, and we know many people, our-- not friends, only people who was a relation to us, one or other way, was killed there. That was terrible. People hide in the basement. And that's something what I mention, maybe you cross already this. Mrs. Melamed mother, Mrs. Birnbaum, what I said-- Melamed go to our side, to-- my father took the family to other side. And we located in a Swedish embassy. And grandmother, I mean Mrs. Birnbaum, and the daughter of Mrs. Melamed-- because there was one grandchild. Mrs. Greenbaum, who is hold this girl. And I not-- we go to special bunker-- called bunker. And we be there all time after German go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? The German go house by house. Was a couple of days, all right? And the-- some guy, who pay money-- a Jewish guy was inside break down, that nerve break, and start holler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because German, who was the special unit, said, if you go out, you are free. We not do nothing for you. You know that's lie. And this fellow said, I like be free, because I don't know who-- something think. I-- and start holler. And German opened this and kill everybody, kill Mrs. Birnbaum, old woman, and also the small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for Melamed, was, you know, big thing, and for us, too. And we know that happened. And it was not big-- the ghetto was destroyed by, not big army, only a couple hundred, you know, guys, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was step-by-step, this house, everything should, you know, burn and something like this. Only couple [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people left from the ghetto. I know-- I don't know how many. And not many. Not many. I know this, because, after the ghetto was destroyed, we lost contact there. We cannot go any more there. And we don't know who was destroyed there. We know five family was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take by my father, you know, different time and locate in Warsaw, in Aryan part.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father take the Melamed family to the embassy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, exactly, I can not-- before the ghetto was destroyed, for sure. Before the ghetto, no question about this. Only, exactly day, I really don't remember. I-- I was involved in this, and I not remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was by carriage. You know, in Warsaw, that's popular before the war and during the war. Special during the war was horse and nice, like you have in New York on the Park Avenue, only nicer not like this. Here is not-- there was, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a very big pleasure, go there and go for a [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was one way. And other way can be only by a rickshaw. You know the rickshaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: And only was transport during evening hours, not night, only the evening hours. Because this was change between the German took the shift during evening hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was very good study, how be there less problem? And transport these people evening by rickshaw or carriage to a-- a place where you like hide. How did he physically get them out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I cannot say you how, because I was on the post watching after the-- these carriages be going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Swedish embassy. I was there. Because you cannot go, bring the people, if are the gendarmes around there. That was my post, there. Generally speaking, the people are prepare and paid him money, through the regular gate. Only the gendarmes take money and turn like this. And you must know which one, gendarmes, doing this, you know, on-- on one thing. And other, some people has the pass. Jewish people who work in the city has pass. That's-- I don't know how my father did this. I cannot explain in detail. Because many people visit us, because-- our factory or our house, because has the paper. Before the war-- before the-- before the ghetto was destroyed, it was a little bit quiet. Because on last couple days, before they were start this problem, that was difficult time. Once, one woman, Mrs. Neufeld daughter-- a very nice person, a very nice girl-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in my-- my father factory asking for a favor-- and not look like Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask for a favor, change for us some dollars, 20 gold dollars. And my father, fine, why not? Only, you must-- I have no money. And I not buy. Only I sell. And it was a couple times this, already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the girl was leaving factory, probably was-- I think so-- was arrested or something like this. The people, the criminal polizei-- policemen, Ukrainian and German, probably arrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. The girl give us address, private address. And the next day, the people stop in factory. They arrest me, because I was on the-- near the desk. Arrest me and said, where you have the gold? What kind gold? I speak the German this time. And took me to the Mercedes, go to my house, and search everything. And you know what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother speak German, not perfect, only, you know, speak enough, because was four years in Germany during the World War First. And took everything what is possible, upside down, and special created my-- searched my room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this girl, day before, left couple gold coins for change. And I supposed to do this not my father. And what I did? You know, I all the time hide the-- if some was the situation, I hide the gold for night or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: This day-- [BANGS FIST]. And the fellows search all. The chair upside down, everything look-- not find nothing. Go to my room. And in my room, in drawer, on the bottom, was the coins. You know, and to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know why. Up to now, I don't know why these people ask me, sign, we find the gold in your house, in your room. You know, with German, it was not-- not so easy, you know? [LAUGHS] I-- I sign those fact, you know? And took the money, took the sign-- I mean the paper, and took me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother start lamented and screaming and asking, please leave. Take everything what you want from house. Give me son back. Meantime, Wanda, Aldona was on the ground there watching what happened. Because, you know, right away, one to other said, there is a revision. Mean is something, search criminal polizei. Because the car was, you know, a criminal polizei car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, we go from the third floor, we go two flight down. And my mother start crying and please, the fellow and the chief there. And happened the fellow was Ukrainian and said, you know what Jewish people did for my family? My mother was, I don't know this-- the Jewish woman, and mean, this woman give me the change. And you know what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This fellow sent all guys down to the car and took the paper, took the money, and said me, go. You know, I go back. That was something there. [SIGHS] Because this was worst, like send me to the concentration-- send me to work someplace. That was, for sure, concentration camp privilege, something like this. That happened to me four-- that was two time arrested, and the third time this, and fourth time, you know, that was Flossenburg. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? How old I was, right? I was about 24 years or 23, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you watched the burning of the ghetto, how many days or weeks did it take? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I not remember. It-- it was not one, two days. That was long enough and further from us. The first day was close to us. Later, there was other part burn. And I am sure it must be at minimum two, three weeks. I not-- I cannot give you the exactly date. And it was-- was job for SS. Mean, that was minimum two weeks, what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's-- I never think that someday I'll be asked for this. [LAUGHS] But I go-- I passed this. Because so many times, so many thing happen to you, you know? You're just-- Did you go down to where the areas that were burning were being destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- you know? Sure. Because this time, during the ghetto, one our-- not my friend, my brother friend, from the same class, was in a unit where has [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a plan and did destroy the wall and let the Jewish people go out and was not satisfy. And our friend-- not our, my brother friend, really, from the same class-- a nice boy, maybe 18, 20 years old, maximum-- was killed, 1 person. There was maybe 30 people involved in this. And one guy was shot by SS. And later, their-- the unit will not leave this guy in one piece and was very bad wounded. Put the grenade and throw below him and was destroyed in the pieces. And wall in the-- hole in the wall was big enough, only people cannot go out, because action was not finished right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your brother's friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wilk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think so. Yuzek, if I remember. Because all this is 50 years now. Yuzek Wilk. Wilk. I don't know the name, what was-- I mean this conspiracy name. Yuzek Wilk. Yeah. And to-- and to also-- in a newspaper here, I saw this. The first time I saw, in a high school, in Locust Valley High School, in newspaper was the memory of Holocaust. And it was this, and Wilk. And I said, my goodness. I speech this. I give the lecture that I can say some [LAUGHS] nice word. And the-- was mentioned Wilk name. And that's-- that's fellow from AK, Armia Krajowa, mean Home Army. That was our obligation, if we can help Jewish people, do this. Because we have-- we fighting against the same animal, German, mean Hitler. That was the reason. We are on the same boat. You know, that that was the feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other thing about the AK, you know how many people they-- was killed because was doing something against the Jewish people? And it was our responsibility to kill them. We'll continue on the next tape.
